---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

**Note:** This is to give you an overview of what an R Mardown Notebook is. (Delete this section once you go through it)

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r}
plot(cars)
```

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press *Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.


# Creates a title (showing how to create a title)

## Creates a second level title (showing how to create a subtitle)

### Creates a third level title (showing how to create a sub-subtitle)


**Note:** Delete the above once you get an overview of R notebook. The next section will be the beginiing of your analysis


# Overview

This analysis file starts from the cleaned analytic dataset created for you:

```text
Data/midus_cancer_aging_analysis_dataset.rds
```

Week 1 Tasks:

1. Install R and RStudio
2. Install the following R packages: tidyverse, gtsummary, flextable, broom, officer, mice, corrr, tibble, misty
3. Create ‘Table 1’ univariate statistics on the study population (put this in a Word document)


The main exposure is standardized perceived cancer risk:

```text
cancerrisk_STD
```

The two outcomes are:

```text
grimage2_STD
dunedinpace_STD
```

To get you started;

# Setup

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Install and load in any required packages in this chunk

# install.packages (c("gtsummary", "tidyverse"))
library(gtsummary)
library(tidyverse)

# this is the theme you will use for the table outputs
theme_gtsummary_journal(journal = "jama")

# Use this to get your working directory
project_dir <- getwd()

# this means all data you create will be saved in the Data folder which is inside this project's working directory
data_dir  <- file.path(project_dir, "Data")

# this means all outputs you create will be saved in the Ouput folder which is inside this project's working directory
output_dir  <- file.path(project_dir, "Output")

```

# Load analytic dataset

```{r load-data}
# This finds the path where the analytic data is stored
analysis_path <- file.path(data_dir, "midus_cancer_aging_analysis_dataset.rds")

# Importing the datset
analysis_df <- readRDS(analysis_path)

# Quick check of the analytic dataset
glimpse(analysis_df)

```



```{r}
# This creates the path where the CSV version will be saved
csv_path <- file.path(data_dir, "midus_cancer_aging_analysis_dataset.csv")

# Export the RDS data as a CSV file
write_csv(analysis_df, csv_path)

```


```{r}
# Create a histogram
histogram_plot <- analysis_df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = income)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Income",
       x = "Income", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))  # this centers the title

# Show the plot in the notebook
histogram_plot

# Save the histogram as a PNG image
ggsave(filename = file.path(output_dir, "example_income_histogram.png"),
       plot = histogram_plot, width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300)
```
